evidence_quotes.quote: Yet, their design often prioritises profit and engagement over user well-being, endangering consumers.

paragraphs.text: Online platforms shape how Europeans communicate, learn, and participate in society. Yet, their design often prioritises profit and engagement over user well-being, endangering consumers. The existing legislation is not designed to incentivise platforms to change this lucrative pattern. The Digital Fairness Act (DFA) offers a crucial opportunity to rebalance this system and ensure fairness, safety, and accountability by design.

section_path: Response to the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns
profiling of minors

paragraphs.text: Despite prohibitions under the Digital Services Act (DSA) against dark patterns, manipulative design, deceptive advertising, untransparent recommender systems, and profiling of minors, these online harms persist. This is hardly surprising considering that online platforms are part of the attention economy and are incentivised to prioritise user engagement over consumer safety. Online harms are consequently systemic problems that require systemic solutions. The DFA must therefore embrace a fairness-by-design approach that embeds adequate safeguards and accountability mechanisms into the system architecture of online platforms.

section_path: Response to the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: manipulative design elements such as streaks, read receipts, autoplay, push notifications, and endless scrolling should be disabled unless explicit user consent is given.
Users need furthermore be able to choose between different recommender algorithms – or opt out of algorithmic curation altogether.
Reporting channels should be user-friendly and transparent, with visible criteria for moderation decisions, ensuring full compliance with Article 16 DSA.
Maximum privacy protection and settings that filter graphic content from user feeds should be preset by default.

paragraphs.text: To reduce the risk of addiction, manipulative design elements such as streaks, read receipts, autoplay, push notifications, and endless scrolling should be disabled unless explicit user consent is given. Interfaces and settings must be clear, accessible, and adjustable at any time. Maximum privacy protection and settings that filter graphic content from user feeds should be preset by default. Users need furthermore be able to choose between different recommender algorithms – or opt out of algorithmic curation altogether. Reporting channels should be user-friendly and transparent, with visible criteria for moderation decisions, ensuring full compliance with Article 16 DSA. Content that violates the law or a platform's terms of service must be easily reportable and swiftly removed.

section_path: Response to the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive algorithms
surveillance-based advertising

paragraphs.text: In addition, policymakers should explore ways to promote alternative models of social media. The Fediverse – a free, decentralised, and open-source network – offers a viable alternative to commercial platforms and operates without surveillance-based advertising or addictive algorithms. So far, these decentralised services could not be established as a real alternative to the existing social media services designated as VLOPs under the DSA. Platforms with a business model that is not build on user attention struggle with funding and feel overburdened with obligations. Moreover, the existing liability regime for hosting providers is not designed to deal with decentralised networks and poses legal challenges and insecurities. By supporting its development, providing guidance and adoption among European users, the EU can mitigate systemic harms and strengthen Europe's digital sovereignty.

section_path: Response to the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Page 1 of 4

section_path: Response to the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

paragraphs.text: Page 2 of 4

section_path: Response to the Public Consultation on the Digital Fairness Act

evidence_quotes.quote: content moderation decisions
account blocks
shadow banning

paragraphs.text: In the digital age, online platforms have become essential spaces for communication, professional activity, and civic participation. At the same time, content moderation decisions, account blocks, shadow banning and other platform actions increasingly affect elected officials and public figures but also journalists, activists, scientists, and ordinary citizens. While European law has established certain rights for platform users, the current legal system often prevents individuals from effectively enforcing these rights. Ensuring access to justice in the digital realm is therefore crucial for safeguarding citizens' rights and maintaining a fair digital public sphere.

evidence_quotes.quote: the platform's terms of use
a jurisdiction clause favouring the platform may take precedence

paragraphs.text: Under the prevailing interpretation of the recast Brussels I Regulation, users who do not qualify as consumers under Article 17(1)(c) cannot rely on consumer jurisdiction. This means that if a platform blocks the account of a politician for example, they are most likely unable to challenge the decision in their home country. Instead, legal action must be brought in the country where the platform is based – typically Ireland. The same may apply if the claim arises from the platform's terms of use, since the place of performance under Article 7(1)(a) is usually deemed to be the platform's seat, or a jurisdiction clause favouring the platform may take precedence. In many instances, users also cannot rely on tort jurisdiction under Article 7(2), for example in cases involving direct infringements of rights, data protection violations, or competition law claims. This problem is not limited to public officials. Journalists, activists, scientists, and ordinary users engaged in professional or mixed-use activities face the same jurisdictional barriers. Even without this problem users are reluctant to litigate against online platforms due to high costs, long proceedings and the imbalance of power between them and giant corporations. High litigation costs, translation requirements, and unfamiliar foreign legal systems make cross-border private claims practically impossible for many users. As a result, their ability to hold platforms accountable for violations of personal rights is severely restricted, undermining the effectiveness of digital rights enforcement through private litigation.

evidence_quotes.quote: European jurisdiction rules must be adapted to reflect the diverse roles of platform users.
close the current gap between the concepts of 'user' and 'consumer'.

paragraphs.text: To address these structural obstacles, European jurisdiction rules must be adapted to reflect the diverse roles of platform users. Users should be able to enforce their rights at their place of residence, in line with Article 79(2) of the GDPR. This could be achieved either through amendments to the Brussels I Regulation or through explicit provisions within the DFA that close the current gap between the concepts of 'user' and 'consumer'.

Additionally, the introduction of digital judicial procedures for violations on online platforms would greatly enhance access to justice. Such procedures should be citizen-friendly, affordable, and efficient, allowing users to challenge account blocks, remove illegal content, or assert other digital rights without prohibitive costs or procedural barriers. By guaranteeing individuals the right to seek justice before their local courts and introducing streamlined digital enforcement mechanisms, the DFA can ensure access to justice for all users.
Page 3 of 4

evidence_quotes.quote: their use therefore needs to be regulated in a proportionate manner – with outright bans considered only as a last resort.

items.topic: Education And Media Literacy For Minors

evidence_quotes.quote: All efforts need to be accompanied by increased education and media literacy that does not only rely on parent's capabilities to prepare minors for the use of the internet.

paragraphs.text: For children and young people, online platforms are an integral part of everyday life. In 2024, 93% of young Germans aged ten and above used social networks, spending an average of 95 minutes per day on their smartphones. While online platforms provide children and young people with valuable opportunities for participation, they also present substantial risks to their mental and physical development. Their use therefore needs to be regulated in a proportionate manner – with outright bans considered only as a last resort. Creating a safe online environment should always be objective. All efforts need to be accompanied by increased education and media literacy that does not only rely on parent's capabilities to prepare minors for the use of the internet.

evidence_quotes.quote: relies solely on self-reported age information
This renders their age verification systems ineffective – often deliberately so.

paragraphs.text: Although online platforms in the EU are formally restricted to users aged 13 and above, minors usually create accounts at an average age of seven to eight. This is possible because most platforms rely solely on self-reported age information. This renders their age verification systems ineffective – often deliberately so. The lack of effective verification is particularly concerning on pornographic platforms. As a result, minors are exposed daily to content that can seriously endanger their mental health, self-image, and social development.

To address this issue, some EU Member States consider imposing blanket bans on social media use for children and teenagers. We regard these attempts as misguided for three main reasons. First, the digital sphere is an important space for minors to express themselves and experience a sense of belonging, especially when offline social interaction is limited (e.g. due to disabilities or queer identity). Second, restricting access does not tackle the underlying systemic harms that make social media risky for users of all ages. In fact, the age group most likely to experience digital violence is not minors, but young adults (aged 18-27). Third, unilateral national efforts at age verification are both legally uncertain and difficult to enforce in practice. The German Network Enforcement Act (NetzDG) offers a cautionary tale in this regard.

evidence_quotes.quote: avoid discrimination or profiling.

paragraphs.text: The right of young people to protection must be balanced with their right to participate in the digital public sphere. Neither the current reliance on self-declared age nor blanket social media bans for minors are adequate solutions. Instead, Member States should establish an effective, harmonised legal framework for mandatory age verification at the European level. Age limits may vary depending on a platform's content, design, and safety features. Technical age verification procedures must adhere strictly to the principles of data minimisation and avoid discrimination or profiling. The use of child protection as a pretext for broader data collection must be explicitly prohibited. To this end, age verification should be carried out by specialised, independent third-party providers, not the platforms themselves. Verification should also not solely rely on identity documents to ensure inclusivity for individuals without, or with provisional, documentation.
Page 4 of 4

The tech industry has been vocal its opposition to the DFA, arguing that "new regulatory layers that wouldn't bring any substantial benefits". Instead, they call for better enforcement of existing legislation. Though the detailed findings of the Digital Fairness Fitness Check clearly contradict the claim that the DFA is unnecessary, the tech industry is right on one point: we do need better rule enforcement.

During the last parliamentary term, the EU introduced pioneering legislation to protect its citizens in the digital age, most notably the DSA, the Digital Markets Act (DMA), and the AI Act (AIA). Though these laws constitute a global milestone in platform accountability, many major platform providers have used every means available to them to push back against their proper implementation. Their tactics include lobbying the US government to put pressure on the EU, mischaracterising the DSA as a censorship law, withdrawing parts of their services from the EU, and simply disregarding inconvenient obligations. By failing to formulate a strong response, the EU enables these platforms to turn its groundbreaking regulations into trivial paper tigers.

By their very nature, laws are only as effective as their enforcement. Enforcement authorities therefore urgently need more financial and human resources to monitor their implementation and ensure compliance. For example, the German Digital Services Coordinator currently operates with around 37 positions, which is less than half of the 99 stipulated by the DSA implementation law. The European Commission thus must ensure that all Members States properly fulfil their obligations arising from Article 50 DSA and Article 70 AIA.

Likewise, the European Commission needs to strengthen its own enforcement capacities. On the one hand, this means properly funding and staffing the responsible enforcement units within DG CNCT and DG COMP. On the other hand, there is a clear need to entangle the enforcement process from the European Commission's role as a political actor. The enforcement of regulation should never be predicated on political negotiations like tariffs. To prevent political interference, an independent enforcement body on the European level is needed. This may be new Regulatory European Agency or even an entirely new EU body that would, like the European Central Bank, exercise its oversight without direct political guidance.

HateAid is a Berlin-based non-profit organisation defending human rights in the digital space. We stand up against digital violence by providing direct counselling and legal support to those affected and fighting its consequences at both social and political levels. HateAid is a designated Trusted Flagger under Article 22 of the DSA. The organisation is led by Managing Directors Anna-Lena von Hodenberg and Josephine Ballon.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
